does: This paper presents a detailed mechanistic explanation of how GPT-2 small solves the indirect object identification task, where models must identify the indirect object in sentences like 'Alice gave a book to Bob' where the answer is Bob rather than Alice. The authors map 26 attention heads organized into 7 functional classes that together implement this behavior, discovered through causal intervention techniques that systematically disable and analyze components. They evaluate their explanation using three quantitative metrics—faithfulness (whether the identified circuit actually produces the behavior), completeness (coverage of the full task), and minimality (whether all components are necessary)—finding that while the circuit explanation is largely reliable, some gaps remain in fully accounting for model performance.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability agenda that has sought to understand neural networks through their internal computational structure, building on prior work that explained simple behaviors like induction heads and in-context learning in language models. The paper responds to the limitation that previous mechanistic interpretability studies either analyzed toy tasks in toy models or provided only high-level descriptions of real behaviors without precise component mapping. It combines multiple interpretability techniques including activation patching and attention pattern analysis, drawing on causal intervention frameworks developed in recent mechanistic interpretability research to move beyond purely correlational analyses.
enables: By demonstrating that a natural language task of meaningful complexity can be reverse-engineered into interpretable computational circuits, this work establishes a proof of concept that mechanistic understanding of larger models may be feasible and worth pursuing systematically. The quantitative evaluation framework for circuit explanations—faithfulness, completeness, and minimality—provides a foundation for future work to develop more rigorous standards for mechanistic interpretability claims. The identified gaps in the explanation suggest that understanding the remaining performance may require discovering additional circuit components, spurring development of more sensitive interpretability tools, and the overall approach could inform scalable reverse-engineering of other behaviors in larger language models.
